questions.question_text: When must a Nebraska licensee provide written agency disclosure?
questions.correct_answer: At first substantial contact or soon after
distractors: At the time a purchase agreement is signed
Within 72 hours of showing a property
Only when requested by the consumer
questions.explanation: Nebraska requires written agency disclosure at the earliest practicable opportunity during or following first substantial contact. This ensures the consumer understands agency relationships before sharing information that could weaken their bargaining position. The requirement applies to a buyer, tenant, seller, or landlord who has not already entered into a written agreement for brokerage services with a designated broker; once a party is represented, other licensees need not repeat the disclosure (Neb. Rev. Stat. 76-2421(1)-(2)).

questions.question_text: A prospective buyer calls a Nebraska agent and begins sharing their maximum budget and financing concerns. What has occurred?
distractors: A binding agency agreement has formed
The agent became a buyer's agent automatically
Nothing requiring disclosure has occurred
questions.explanation: First substantial contact occurs when a licensee begins to elicit or accept compromising information, meaning information that could reduce a party's bargaining power. Sharing a maximum budget and financing details qualifies as compromising information.

questions.question_text: In Nebraska, 'compromising information' in the context of agency disclosure refers to details that could reduce a party's what?
distractors: Credit score
Property value
Closing timeline
questions.explanation: Compromising information is defined as information that reduces a party's bargaining power. Examples include a buyer's maximum price, a seller's urgency to sell, or either party's financial constraints. Disclosure of agency must occur before such information is exchanged.

questions.question_text: A Nebraska licensee meets a walk-in buyer at an open house and discusses the buyer's pre-approval amount. The licensee has not presented the Agency Disclosure Pamphlet. What violation has occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Failure to disclose agency at first substantial contact
distractors: No violation until a written offer is made
A violation only if the buyer signs a contract
No violation because open houses are exempt
questions.explanation: Discussing a buyer's pre-approval amount means eliciting or accepting compromising information, which is 'specific assistance' marking first substantial contact under NREC Policy & Interpretation #38 — open houses are not exempt. The licensee must, at the earliest practicable opportunity, provide the commission-approved brokerage disclosure pamphlet and written disclosure and obtain the buyer's signed acknowledgment of receipt (or note the buyer's refusal to sign on a retained copy) (Neb. Rev. Stat. 76-2421(1), (5)).

questions.question_text: Under Neb. Rev. Stat. 76-2421, what must the licensee's written disclosure at first substantial contact identify?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage relationships offered or which party the licensee represents
distractors: Commission rates charged by the brokerage
The licensee's sales volume and experience
Comparable property values in the area
questions.explanation: At the earliest practicable opportunity during or following first substantial contact, the licensee must give the consumer the commission-approved brokerage disclosure pamphlet and must disclose in writing either the types of brokerage relationships the designated broker and affiliated licensees are offering to that person or which party the licensee is representing (Neb. Rev. Stat. 76-2421(1)). The disclosure includes a signature block for the consumer to acknowledge receipt; commission rates, sales records, and comparable values are not required.

questions.question_text: In a Nebraska cooperative transaction, when must the selling broker deposit earnest money into the trust account?
questions.correct_answer: Within 72 hours or by the end of the next banking day after written acceptance
distractors: Within 24 hours of receiving the check
Within 5 business days of receiving the check
Only after the transaction closes
questions.explanation: Title 299 NAC ch. 3-007 requires the selling broker in a cooperative sale — unless all parties to the transaction with an interest in the funds agree otherwise in writing — to deposit the earnest money into the selling broker's trust account within 72 hours or before the end of the next banking day after the offer is accepted in writing, and then forthwith transfer it to the listing broker by a check drawn on the selling broker's trust account. The deadline runs from written acceptance of the offer, not from receipt of the funds.

questions.question_text: A selling broker in Nebraska receives a $5,000 earnest money check on Monday. After depositing it, what must happen next?
questions.correct_answer: Transfer the funds to the listing broker
distractors: Hold the funds until closing
Send the funds directly to the title company
Return the check to the buyer for safekeeping
questions.explanation: Under Title 299 NAC ch. 3-007, the selling broker in a cooperative transaction deposits the earnest money into the selling broker's trust account within 72 hours or before the end of the next banking day after written acceptance, then forthwith transfers it to the listing broker by a check drawn on the selling broker's trust account (unless all parties with an interest in the funds have agreed otherwise in writing, such as directing the deposit to a title company). The listing broker holds the funds in trust until closing or other disposition.

questions.question_text: In a Nebraska cooperative sale, the listing broker represents the seller and the selling broker represents the buyer. Who is responsible for disclosing agency to the buyer?
distractors: The listing broker
Both brokers jointly
The seller's attorney
questions.explanation: Each broker in a cooperative transaction discloses agency to the parties they interact with. Since the selling broker works directly with the buyer, that broker is responsible for providing the buyer with proper agency disclosure.

questions.question_text: Two Nebraska brokers cooperate on a sale but never discussed how the commission would be split. A dispute arises at closing. What best prevents this situation?
distractors: The MLS automatically sets the split
State law mandates a 50/50 division
The title company decides at closing
questions.explanation: Commission sharing in cooperative transactions must be agreed upon and disclosed. While MLS listings may offer a cooperating broker compensation, a written agreement between brokers before closing prevents disputes and meets disclosure requirements.

questions.question_text: In a cooperative transaction, a Nebraska selling broker shows the property but represents the buyer. Whom does the listing broker represent?
distractors: The buyer through cooperation
Both parties as a dual agent
Neither party as a facilitator
questions.explanation: In cooperative transactions, the listing broker and selling broker may represent different parties. The listing broker represents the seller under the listing agreement, while the selling broker in this scenario represents the buyer under a buyer agency agreement.

questions.question_text: A Nebraska licensee wants to purchase a property listed by their own brokerage. What must the licensee do?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the personal interest in writing
distractors: Have another agent handle the entire sale
Wait until the listing expires to make an offer
Obtain approval from the real estate commission
questions.explanation: A licensee buying a property listed with their own brokerage is both a principal and connected to the agency side of the transaction. Acting as agent and undisclosed principal in any transaction is an unfair trade practice under Neb. Rev. Stat. 81-885.24(9), so the licensee's personal interest must be disclosed to all parties — in practice, in writing in the purchase agreement — to ensure transparency.

questions.question_text: A Nebraska agent's spouse owns a property that the agent lists for sale without disclosing the family relationship. What consequence may result?
distractors: No consequence if the price is fair
A fine only if the buyer complains
Automatic license revocation
questions.explanation: Concealing a spousal ownership interest hides the licensee's indirect stake in the transaction. NREC may discipline the licensee under Neb. Rev. Stat. 81-885.24 — for example as acting as agent and undisclosed principal (subdivision (9)), a substantial misrepresentation (22), or conduct demonstrating unworthiness (29) — regardless of whether the transaction's price was fair. Discipline may range from censure or civil fine to suspension or revocation; revocation is not automatic.

questions.question_text: A Nebraska broker has a financial interest in a title company and refers clients there. What is required?
distractors: Referrals to affiliated businesses are prohibited
No disclosure if the title company is reputable
Written commission approval from NREC
questions.explanation: Accepting any undisclosed compensation, rebate, or direct profit on expenditures made for a principal is an unfair trade practice (Neb. Rev. Stat. 81-885.24(6)), and referrals to a settlement-service provider in which the broker has an ownership interest also trigger the federal RESPA affiliated-business-arrangement disclosure. The referral itself is permitted; the financial relationship must be disclosed to the client.

questions.question_text: A licensee fails to disclose that they are selling their own investment property through their brokerage. Which category of violation does this represent?
questions.correct_answer: A conflict of interest requiring disclosure
distractors: An unauthorized practice of law issue
A prohibited commingling of funds
A violation of Nebraska fair housing law
questions.explanation: Selling one's own property through the brokerage without disclosure is a conflict of interest violation. The licensee has a personal financial stake that could influence their conduct, and all parties must be informed of this dual role.

questions.question_text: Which of the following financial relationships must a Nebraska licensee disclose to clients?
questions.correct_answer: Ownership interest in a referred service provider
distractors: The licensee's personal mortgage balance
The brokerage's annual revenue
Commission earned on previous transactions
questions.explanation: Nebraska requires disclosure of financial interests that create conflicts, such as owning part of a company to which clients are referred. Personal financial details unrelated to the transaction, like the licensee's mortgage or brokerage revenue, are not required disclosures.
